prompts.title: Nightly Memory Consolidation
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that runs every night at 3 AM to consolidate daily memory files into long-term storage. Finds all daily memory files from the past 24 hours, merges unique entries into your main long-term memory file, deduplicates by content match, and logs the results. Alerts you via Telegram when complete.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that runs every night at 3 AM to consolidate daily memory files into long-term storage.

OBJECTIVE: Find all daily memory files created in the past 24 hours, read their contents, merge unique entries into the main long-term memory file, deduplicate by exact content match, and log the results.

DIRECTORIES:
- Daily memories: memory/daily/*.md
- Main long-term memory: memory/longterm.md
- Log file: memory/consolidation.log

PROCESS:
1. List all files in memory/daily/ modified in the last 24 hours using exec with 'find memory/daily/ -type f -mtime -1'
2. Read each daily file using filesystem
3. Load existing longterm.md
4. For each entry in daily files, check if it already exists in longterm.md (by exact content match)
5. Append only new unique entries to longterm.md
6. Archive processed daily files to memory/archive/YYYY-MM-DD.bak
7. Write consolidation summary to memory/consolidation.log

OUTPUT: A consolidation report: timestamp, files processed, entries examined, new entries added, duplicates skipped, any errors.

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem

ALERT: After each run, send a brief Telegram message: 'Memory consolidation complete: X new entries merged, Y duplicates skipped.'
setup_steps: Create directories: memory/daily, memory/archive, memory/
Create longterm.md with header: "# Long-term Memory" if it doesn't exist
Create an empty consolidation.log file
Add to crontab: "0 3 * * *" pointing to the consolidation script
Test by running with sample daily files first
Verify log rotation for consolidation.log
prompts.example_output:
=== Consolidation 2026-04-08 ===
Files: 2 processed
Entries: 34 examined
Added: 12 new
Skipped: 22 duplicates
Next run: 2026-04-09 03:00

prompts.title: Heartbeat Monitor
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that pings a configurable list of service URLs every 5 minutes and immediately alerts you via Telegram when any service goes down. Logs all results with timestamps. Handles timeouts, DNS failures, and SSL errors as failures.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that pings a configurable list of service URLs every 5 minutes and alerts you immediately when any service goes down.

CONFIGURATION:
- services.txt: one URL per line (e.g., https://api.yoursite.com/health)
- log file: ~/logs/heartbeat.log

PROCESS:
1. Read all URLs from services.txt
2. For each URL, run: curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -L --max-time 10 [URL]
3. Log each result with timestamp to heartbeat.log: '[TIME] | [URL] | [STATUS] | [RESPONSE_MS]ms'
4. If status != 200, immediately send Telegram alert via message tool

ALERT FORMAT:
"🚨 Service Down: [URL] returned [STATUS] at [TIME]
Response time: [X]ms"

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem, message

The script must handle connection timeouts (status 000), DNS failures, and SSL errors as failures.
setup_steps: Create services.txt with one URL per line
Create script: ~/scripts/heartbeat.sh that loops through services.txt
Make script executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/heartbeat.sh
Add to crontab: "*/5 * * * *" ~/scripts/heartbeat.sh
Test manually: run script and verify log output
Verify Telegram bot is configured in OpenClaw message tool
prompts.example_output:
14:30:01 | https://api.example.com | 200 | 45ms | OK
14:30:01 | https://db.example.com | 200 | 23ms | OK
14:30:02 | https://backup.example.com | 000 | 0ms | TIMEOUT
[TG ALERT] 🚨 Service Down: https://backup.example.com returned 000 at 14:30:02

prompts.title: Daily Channel Digest
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that runs every morning at 8 AM, retrieves the previous 24 hours of messages from your Slack or Discord channel, summarizes key discussions and action items, and sends a digest to your Telegram.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that runs every morning at 8 AM, retrieves the previous 24 hours of messages from your configured Slack or Discord channel, summarizes the key discussions, decisions, and action items, and sends a digest to your Telegram.

PROCESS:
1. Use sessions_list to identify active messaging sessions
2. Use sessions_history to fetch messages from the target channel for the last 24 hours
3. Identify: key topics discussed, decisions made, action items assigned, unanswered questions
4. Format as a clean digest: sectioned by topic, bullet points, owner assigned where visible
5. Send digest to Telegram via message tool

TOOLS: sessions_list, sessions_history, message, cron
setup_steps: Configure your Slack/Discord channel bindings in OpenClaw
Identify the session key for the channel you want to digest
Create a script that fetches 24h of history and formats the digest
Set cron: "0 8 * * *" to run the digest script each morning
Test with --dry-run first to verify correct channel and output format
Verify Telegram bot can send to your chat ID

prompts.title: Disk Space Alert System
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that checks disk usage on all mounted volumes every 10 minutes and alerts you via Telegram when any volume exceeds 85% capacity. Logs all readings. Critical alerts at 90%.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that checks disk usage on all mounted volumes every 10 minutes and alerts you via Telegram when any volume exceeds 85% capacity.

PROCESS:
1. Run: df -h (excluding tmpfs/snap/virtual filesystems)
2. Parse output to extract: filesystem, total size, used space, usage percentage
3. For each volume, check if usage % > 85
4. Log all readings to ~/logs/disk_usage.log with timestamp
5. If any volume > 85%, immediately send Telegram alert

ALERT FORMAT:
"💾 Disk Alert: [VOLUME] at [USAGE]%
Total: [SIZE] | Used: [USED] | Available: [AVAIL]
Time: [TIMESTAMP]"

For volumes > 90%: escalate with '🔴 CRITICAL' prefix.

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem, message
setup_steps: Create log directory: mkdir -p ~/logs
Create script: ~/scripts/disk_check.sh that runs df -h and parses output
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/disk_check.sh
Add to crontab: "*/10 * * * *" ~/scripts/disk_check.sh
Test manually: run script and check log file output
Verify Telegram bot is working before enabling alert mode
prompts.example_output:
14:40:01 | /dev/disk1s1 | 67% | 912GB/1TB | OK
14:40:01 | /dev/disk1s2 | 91% | 455GB/500GB | 🔴 CRITICAL
[TG ALERT] 💾 Disk Alert: /dev/disk1s2 at 91%. Available: 45GB

prompts.title: Session Health Monitor
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that checks the health of all OpenClaw sessions every 15 minutes, identifies stale or unresponsive sessions, recovers or archives them, and sends you a Telegram digest of what was found.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that checks the health of all OpenClaw sessions every 15 minutes, identifies stale or unresponsive sessions, and either recovers or archives them.

PROCESS:
1. Use sessions_list to retrieve all active sessions with their last activity timestamps
2. For each session, determine if last activity > 30 minutes ago
3. For sessions inactive > 30 min: use sessions_send with a lightweight echo to test responsiveness
4. If no response within 10 seconds: mark as stale
5. For stale sessions: log to ~/logs/stale_sessions.log
6. Archive stale sessions using exec to remove their session files from ~/.openclaw/sessions/
7. Send a summary digest to Telegram

THRESHOLD: 30 minutes of inactivity = stale

TOOLS: sessions_list, sessions_send, exec, filesystem, message, cron
setup_steps: Verify sessions_list returns all expected sessions
Create ~/logs/ directory for session logs
Create script: ~/scripts/session_health.sh following the PROCESS above
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/session_health.sh
Add to crontab: "*/15 * * * *" ~/scripts/session_health.sh
Test manually with --dry-run first to verify correct session paths
Set up Telegram bot for alert delivery
prompts.example_output:
=== Session Health 14:45:01 ===
Active sessions:
  agent:main:session-abc123 (active 2m ago) ✓
  agent:main:session-def456 (active 8m ago) ✓
Stale sessions:
  agent:main:session-xyz789 (inactive 47m) → archived
[TG DIGEST] 2 stale sessions archived. No unresponsive sessions.

prompts.title: Automated Git Commit Watcher
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that watches a specific directory for file changes every 10 minutes and automatically commits any uncommitted changes to git with a timestamped commit message listing exactly which files changed. Never auto-commits to main.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that watches a specific directory (e.g., ~/projects/myapp) for file changes every 10 minutes, and automatically commits any uncommitted changes to git with a timestamped commit message listing exactly which files changed.

PROCESS:
1. Run: cd ~/projects/myapp && git status --porcelain to get list of changed files
2. If no changes: exit silently
3. If changes exist:
   a. git add -A
   b. git commit -m '[AUTOMATED] Updates $(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H:%M): Changed files: [file1], [file2], ...'
   c. git push origin [current-branch]
4. Log commit details to ~/logs/git_auto_commit.log
5. Send Telegram confirmation with file list

EXCLUDE PATTERNS:
- node_modules/
- .git/
- *.log
- .env
- build/dist/ directories

BRANCH HANDLING: Only commit to development or feature branches. Never auto-commit to main/master.

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem, message
setup_steps: Navigate to ~/projects/myapp and verify git repo is initialized
Create .gitignore with EXCLUDE PATTERNS above
Create script: ~/scripts/git_watcher.sh following the PROCESS above
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/git_watcher.sh
Add to crontab: "*/10 * * * *" ~/scripts/git_watcher.sh
Test manually: make a change, run script, verify commit appears in git log
Verify Telegram bot is configured for commit notifications
prompts.example_output:
=== Git Watcher 14:50:01 ===
Changes detected:
  M src/index.js
  M src/utils.js
  A tests/new_test.js
Commit: [AUTOMATED] Updates 2026-04-08_14:50: Changed files: src/index.js, src/utils.js, tests/new_test.js
Branch: development
Pushed: origin/development
[TG] ✅ Auto-commit: 3 files pushed to dev

prompts.title: Nightly Backup Automation
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that runs every night at 2 AM, creates a compressed timestamped backup of your critical directories (workspace, memory, config, scripts), stores it locally, verifies with checksum, and sends you a confirmation via Telegram. Auto-deletes backups older than 30 days.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that runs every night at 2 AM, creates a compressed timestamped backup of your critical directories, stores it locally with a dated filename, and sends you a confirmation when complete.

DIRECTORIES TO BACKUP:
- ~/.openclaw/workspace/
- ~/.openclaw/memory/
- ~/.openclaw/config/
- ~/scripts/

PROCESS:
1. Create backup directory: ~/backups/
2. Create filename: backup_YYYY-MM-DD.tar.gz
3. Run: tar -czf ~/backups/backup_YYYY-MM-DD.tar.gz [directories above]
4. Verify backup with: tar -tzf filename.tar.gz | wc -l (confirm file count)
5. Calculate checksum: shasum -a 256 filename.tar.gz
6. Log backup details to ~/logs/backups.log
7. Send Telegram confirmation with backup size, file count, and checksum

RETENTION: Delete backups older than 30 days automatically.

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem, message
setup_steps: Create ~/backups/ directory
Create ~/logs/ directory if not exists
Create script: ~/scripts/nightly_backup.sh following the PROCESS above
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/nightly_backup.sh
Test manually: run script, verify backup file in ~/backups/
Add to crontab: "0 2 * * *" ~/scripts/nightly_backup.sh
Verify Telegram notifications are working
prompts.example_output:
=== Nightly Backup 02:00:15 ===
Directories: workspace, memory, config, scripts
Backup: ~/backups/backup_2026-04-08.tar.gz
Size: 234MB
Files archived: 1,847
Checksum: a3f8d2c9...
Retention: 2 old backups purged
[TG] ✅ Backup complete: backup_2026-04-08.tar.gz (234MB, 1,847 files)

prompts.title: OpenClaw Access Counter
prompts.description: Set up an always-on automation that tracks how often each of your memory files is accessed. Every read gets logged. Runs a daily analysis that identifies your most-accessed and never-accessed files, so you know what to promote to long-term memory and what to archive. Results sent to Telegram.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that tracks how often each of your memory files is accessed. Every time a memory file is read, append an entry to an access log. Run a daily analysis that identifies your most-accessed and never-accessed files, so you can decide what to promote to long-term memory and what to archive.

LOG FORMAT (access_log.jsonl):
{"timestamp": "ISO8601", "file": "memory/path/to/file.md", "action": "read", "access_count": 1}

PROCESS:
1. Create access_log.jsonl in memory/ directory
2. Create a tracking wrapper script that: reads target memory file, increments access count for that file in access_log.jsonl, returns content
3. For each read operation on memory files, use the wrapper instead of direct filesystem reads
4. Daily at midnight: run analysis on access_log.jsonl
5. Generate report: files accessed >10 times this week (hot), files accessed 0 times in 30 days (cold)
6. Send report to Telegram

ANALYSIS:
- Hot files (>10 accesses): promote to long-term memory
- Cold files (0 accesses in 30 days): consider archiving

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create memory/access_log.jsonl with header: {"access_log": []}
Create tracking script: ~/scripts/memory_tracker.sh
Update relevant memory read operations to use the tracker wrapper
Create analysis script: ~/scripts/memory_stats.sh for midnight analysis
Add to crontab: "0 0 * * *" for daily analysis
Test by manually triggering analysis and checking output
Verify Telegram bot receives the daily report
prompts.example_output:
=== Memory Access Stats - Apr 8 ===
HOT (most accessed this week):
  memory/project-a.md: 47 reads
  memory/todos.md: 31 reads
  memory/contacts.md: 22 reads

COLD (not accessed in 30 days):
  memory/old-ideas-2025.md: 0 reads
  memory/archived-notes.md: 0 reads

Recommendation: Archive cold files. Promote hot files to long-term memory.

prompts.title: Keyword Sentiment Tracker
prompts.description: Monitors Reddit, Twitter/X, and web mentions of any keyword you specify, analyzes sentiment across sources, and sends a daily summary to Telegram with volume trends and top discussions.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that monitors mentions of a keyword across Reddit, Twitter/X, and the web — then sends you a daily sentiment digest on Telegram.

CONFIGURATION:
Create a config file at ~/configs/sentiment_config.json:
{
  "keyword": "YOUR_KEYWORD",
  "subreddits": ["relevant1", "relevant2"],
  "twitter_handles": ["handle1", "handle2"],
  "telegram_chat_id": "YOUR_CHAT_ID"
}

PROCESS:
1. REDDIT SEARCH: Use web_search to query "site:reddit.com KEYWORD" and also "KEYWORD reddit" — retrieve top 5 results from the last 7 days
2. TWITTER/X SEARCH: Use web_search to query "KEYWORD site:x.com OR site:twitter.com since:2026-01-01" — retrieve top 10 tweets/reposts
3. GENERAL WEB: Use web_search to query "KEYWORD" (no site filter) — get top 5 articles from the last 7 days
4. For each source, read the page/content using web_fetch to extract:
   - Reddit: post title, score, number of comments, subreddit, flaired sentiment if visible
   - Twitter: tweet text, likes, retweets, reply count
   - Web: article headline, publication, key pull quotes

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS (manual classification):
Classify each mention as POSITIVE, NEGATIVE, or NEUTRAL based on:
- POSITIVE: praise, excitement, recommendations, problem solved, ROI, growth
- NEGATIVE: complaints, bugs, outages, failures, regrets, cancellations, scams
- NEUTRAL: news articles, announcements, how-to guides, comparisons without opinion

Score: POSITIVE = +1, NEGATIVE = -1, NEUTRAL = 0
Sentiment Score = sum(scores) / total_mentions (range -1 to +1)

DAILY DIGEST FORMAT (send via message tool to Telegram):
📊 *Keyword Sentiment Report — [DATE]*

🔍 *Volume:* X mentions today (vs Y yesterday)
😊/😐/😠 *Sentiment:* X% positive / X% neutral / X% negative
📈 *Score:* X.XX (range -1 to +1)

*Top Positive Mentions:*
• [Source] — [1-2 sentence summary]

*Top Negative Mentions:*
• [Source] — [1-2 sentence summary]

*Key Discussions:*
• [Source] — [1-sentence takeaway]

TOOLS: web_search, web_fetch, exec, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/ directory and sentiment_config.json with your keyword, target subreddits, and Twitter handles
Create a script at ~/scripts/sentiment_tracker.sh that runs the full monitoring pipeline
Make the script executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/sentiment_tracker.sh
Add to crontab: "0 9 * * *" to run every morning at 9 AM (adjust as needed)
Test manually: run the script with a test keyword and verify the Telegram digest looks correct
Verify your Telegram bot can send to the configured chat ID
prompts.example_output:

🔍 *Volume:* 23 mentions today (vs 18 yesterday)
😊/😐/😠 *Sentiment:* 52% positive / 30% neutral / 18% negative
📈 *Score:* +0.34

*Top Positive Mentions:*

prompts.title: Competitor Change Detector
prompts.description: Watches a competitor website or blog and notifies you via Telegram when they publish new content, change pricing, or update key pages — keeping you ahead of market moves.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that watches a competitor website or blog and notifies you via Telegram when they publish new content or change pricing.

CONFIGURATION:
Create ~/configs/competitor_config.json:
{
  "competitors": [
    {
      "name": "CompetitorName",
      "blog_url": "https://competitor.com/blog",
      "pricing_url": "https://competitor.com/pricing",
      "sitemap_url": "https://competitor.com/sitemap.xml"
    }
  ],
  "check_interval_hours": 6,
  "state_file": "~/.openclaw/competitor_state.json"
}

STATE FILE:
Track last known state to detect changes:
{
  "competitor.com/blog": {
    "last_urls": ["article-1", "article-2"],
    "last_check": 1744156800,
    "pricing_hash": "sha256_of_pricing_page"
  }
}

PROCESS:
1. Read the competitor sitemap using web_fetch to get all blog post URLs
2. Filter to posts published or updated in the last 7 days
3. Compare against state_file last known URLs
4. NEW CONTENT: For any new posts found:
   a. Fetch the article with web_fetch
   b. Extract: title, publish date, excerpt (first 200 chars), estimated read time
   c. Classify content type: announcement, tutorial, comparison, pricing update, case study
5. PRICING CHANGES: For pricing_url:
   a. Fetch page and compute SHA-256 hash of the raw text
   b. Compare to stored hash in state_file
   c. If changed: fetch the full page and extract what changed (added/removed features, price changes)
6. Update state_file with new URLs and hashes
7. If changes found: send Telegram alert immediately

ALERT FORMAT (message tool to Telegram):
🔍 *Competitor Alert: [CompetitorName]*

📝 *New Content ({count}):*
• [Post Title](URL) — {type} — {excerpt}

{If pricing changed:}
💰 *Pricing Update:*
{Before: bullet of old pricing} → {After: bullet of new pricing}

🕐 *Checked:* [timestamp]

TOOLS: web_fetch, exec, cron, message, filesystem
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/competitor_config.json with your competitor URLs and check interval
Create the state file at ~/.openclaw/competitor_state.json (initially empty dict {})
Create script: ~/scripts/competitor_check.sh that runs the full pipeline
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/competitor_check.sh
Add to crontab: "0 */6 * * *" to check every 6 hours
Test manually first: run with --verbose and check output
Verify Telegram bot is configured
prompts.example_output:
🔍 *Competitor Alert: ProtocolX*

📝 *New Content (2):*
• [How We Reduced Gas Fees by 40%](https://protocolx.com/blog/gas-optimization) — tutorial — Learn the exact infrastructure changes that cut our users gas costs significantly...
• [New Enterprise Tier Announced](https://protocolx.com/blog/enterprise-tier) — announcement — Introducing ProtocolX Enterprise with dedicated support and SLA guarantees.

💰 *Pricing Update:*
Before: Pro @ /mo | Team @ /mo
After: Pro @ /mo | Team @ /mo (+33% increase)

🕐 *Checked: Apr 11 2026 14:00 UTC*

prompts.title: ArXiv Paper Alert
prompts.description: Watches arXiv for new papers matching keywords you care about, sends abstracts to Telegram with link, authors, and relevance score so you never miss a relevant paper.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that watches arXiv for new papers matching keywords you care about, sends abstracts to Telegram with link, authors, and relevance score.

CONFIGURATION:
Create ~/configs/arxiv_config.json:
{
  "keywords": ["decentralized finance", "layer 2 scaling", "zero knowledge proofs"],
  "categories": ["cs.CR", "cs.LO", "q-fin.TR"],
  "max_results_per_keyword": 3,
  "state_file": "~/.openclaw/arxiv_state.json"
}

STATE FILE:
Track papers already sent to avoid duplicates:
{
  "sent_ids": ["2301.12345", "2301.12346"],
  "last_check": 1744156800
}

PROCESS:
1. For each keyword in config, construct arXiv search URL:
   https://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=all:KEYWORD&start=0&max_results=10&sortBy=submittedDate&sortOrder=descending
2. Fetch the RSS/XML feed using web_fetch
3. Parse entries: for each paper, extract:
   - ID (from arXiv ID in entry title/link)
   - Title
   - Authors (first 3 + "et al." if more)
   - Published date
   - Summary/abstract (first 300 characters)
   - arXiv URL
4. Filter: only papers from the last 7 days
5. For each new paper:
   a. Check if ID is in state_file sent_ids — skip if already sent
   b. Calculate relevance score (1-10): count keyword appearances in title + abstract
   c. If relevance score >= 5, flag as HIGH priority
6. Update state_file with new paper IDs
7. Send Telegram message

ALERT FORMAT (message tool to Telegram):
📚 *ArXiv Alert: [KEYWORD]*

*{count} new papers (last 7 days)*

🔬 *[Paper Title]*(URL)
Authors: [First Author et al.] | {relevance: ★★★★★}
{first 280 chars of abstract}...

🕐 *Submitted:* YYYY-MM-DD

TOOLS: web_fetch, exec, cron, message, filesystem
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/arxiv_config.json with your target keywords and arXiv categories
Initialize ~/.openclaw/arxiv_state.json with {"sent_ids": [], "last_check": 0}
Create script: ~/scripts/arxiv_check.sh that fetches and parses the arXiv RSS feed
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/arxiv_check.sh
Add to crontab: "0 8,20 * * *" to check twice daily (8 AM and 8 PM)
Test: run script manually and verify it fetches and parses paper entries correctly
Verify Telegram bot is working
prompts.example_output:
📚 *ArXiv Alert: zero knowledge proofs*

*3 new papers (last 7 days)*

🔬 *[Zero-Knowledge Succinct Non-Interactive Arguments for L1 Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.12345)*
Authors: Chen, Wang et al. | {relevance: ★★★★★}
We present a new construction of zk-SNARKs with logarithmic proof size and constant-time verification, achieving significant improvements over prior work...

🕐 *Submitted: 2026-04-08*

prompts.title: Daily Tech Digest
prompts.description: Pulls top crypto/AI news from multiple sources every morning, summarizes into a clean 5-bullet brief with sources, and sends to Telegram — your daily competitive intelligence in 2 minutes.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that runs every morning, pulls the top crypto and AI news from multiple sources, summarizes into a 5-bullet brief, and sends it to Telegram.

SOURCES TO CHECK:
- Crypto: CoinDesk (coindesk.com), The Block (theblock.co), CryptoSlate (cryptoslate.com)
- AI: The Verge AI (theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence), TechCrunch AI (techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence), VentureBeat AI (venturebeat.com/category/ai)

PROCESS:
1. For each source:
   a. Use web_search to find top article: "site:coindesk.com 2026" and "site:theblock.co 2026"
   b. Use web_fetch to fetch the article text for top 1-2 articles per source
   c. Extract: headline, 2-sentence summary, key quote, publication time
2. ALSO search for breaking news:
   a. web_search: "crypto news today" (top 3)
   b. web_search: "AI artificial intelligence news today" (top 3)
3. Combine all findings — deduplicate by headline similarity
4. Select top 5 stories ranked by:
   - Breaking/urgent (announcements, launches, hacks) = high priority
   - Market-moving news (regulatory, major partnerships) = high priority
   - Industry trends (new research, significant product updates) = medium priority
5. Format the digest

DIGEST FORMAT (send via message tool to Telegram):
📰 *Daily Tech Digest — [DATE]*

*Top [N] Stories:*

1️⃣ *[Headline]*(URL)
   {2-sentence summary} — *Source*

2️⃣ *[Headline]*(URL)
   {2-sentence summary} — *Source*

... (up to 5)

*Quick Takes:*
• {One sentence hot take or context}

TOOLS: web_search, web_fetch, cron, message
setup_steps: Verify web_search returns fresh results for each source
Create script: ~/scripts/daily_digest.sh that automates the full pipeline
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/daily_digest.sh
Add to crontab: "0 9 * * *" to run every morning at 9 AM
Test: run manually and verify digest format looks good
Verify Telegram bot can receive the digest with clickable links
prompts.example_output:
📰 *Daily Tech Digest — Apr 11 2026*

prompts.title: Eisenhower Todo Prioritizer
prompts.description: Takes your TASKS.md or any task list and reorders it using the urgent/important matrix — tells you what to do first, what to schedule, what to delegate, and what to cut.
prompts.prompt_text:
Take a task list (from TASKS.md or pasted text) and reorder it using the Eisenhower Matrix — urgent/important grid. Tell you exactly what to do first, what to schedule, what to delegate, and what to eliminate.

EISENHOWER MATRIX DEFINITIONS:
- QUADRANT 1 (Urgent + Important): Do immediately — crises, deadlines, emergencies
- QUADRANT 2 (Not Urgent + Important): Schedule — planning, relationships, growth
- QUADRANT 3 (Urgent + Not Important): Delegate — interruptions, others priorities
- QUADRANT 4 (Not Urgent + Not Important): Eliminate — timewasters, trivial, escape

INPUT:
Read TASKS.md from ~/.openclaw/workspace/TASKS.md or use tasks provided by the user.

PROCESS:
1. Parse all tasks — each line is a task (bullet points, numbered lists, or plain text)
2. For each task, classify by:
   - Urgency: Does it have a deadline? Is it blocking others? Is it time-sensitive?
   - Importance: Does it align with top goals? Does it have high impact if done?
3. Assign each task to a quadrant
4. Reorder the task list accordingly
5. If a task is ambiguous, ask the user to clarify urgency or importance

OUTPUT:
Display the full Eisenhower Matrix with tasks grouped:

🟥 QUADRANT 1 — DO NOW (Urgent + Important)
  1. [Task text] — deadline: [date if known]
  2. [Task text] — why urgent: [1 sentence]

🟨 QUADRANT 2 — SCHEDULE (Important, Not Urgent)
  1. [Task text] — suggested deadline: [3-5 days from now]
  2. [Task text] — why important: [1 sentence]

🟩 QUADRANT 3 — DELEGATE (Urgent, Not Important)
  1. [Task text] — can someone else handle this?

🟦 QUADRANT 4 — ELIMINATE (Neither)
  1. [Task text] — reason: [why this can be dropped]

Also output a reprioritized task list (top to bottom):
1. Do first: [Q1 tasks in order]
2. Then: [Q2 tasks in order]
3. Consider: [Q3 tasks]
4. Drop: [Q4 tasks]

TOOLS: read, exec
setup_steps: Point to your TASKS.md: ~/.openclaw/workspace/TASKS.md
Or paste/send your raw task list as input
Model will parse, classify, and return the full Eisenhower Matrix
Review Q1 tasks — those are your immediate priorities
For Q2 tasks: set concrete deadlines to prevent slippage
prompts.example_output:
🟥 QUADRANT 1 — DO NOW
  1. Submit tax extension — deadline: Apr 15
  2. Fix production bug in swap module — blocking 3 other devs

🟨 QUADRANT 2 — SCHEDULE
  1. Write Q2 content calendar — blocks strategy meeting
  2. Review competitor pricing — informs next roadmap decision

🟩 QUADRANT 3 — DELEGATE
  1. Update investor deck slides — assistant can draft

🟦 QUADRANT 4 — ELIMINATE
  1. Refactor legacy auth module — not blocking anything

prompts.title: Memory Decay Detector
prompts.description: Flags memory files untouched for 30+ days, suggests what is stale and ready to archive — keeps your knowledge base lean and relevant.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that scans your memory files, flags anything untouched for 30+ days, and suggests what is stale and ready to archive.

CONFIGURATION:
- memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/
- state_file: ~/.openclaw/memory/memory_decay_state.json
- threshold_days: 30

STATE FILE FORMAT:
{
  "files": {
    "memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md": {
      "last_modified": 1744156800,
      "last_accessed": 1744156800,
      "word_count": 450,
      "access_count": 3
    }
  },
  "archive_candidates": []
}

PROCESS:
1. List all files in ~/.openclaw/memory/ recursively (/*.md and /*/*.md)
2. For each file, get: last modified timestamp (stat -c %Y), file size, word count (wc -w)
3. Load state_file — if file is new, add entry. If file was previously seen, update.
4. For each file, calculate days_since_access = (now - last_modified) / 86400
5. Flag files where days_since_access > 30 as STALE
6. Also flag files with word_count < 50 and access_count == 1 as LOW_VALUE
7. Build archive_candidates list sorted by: (days_since_access DESC, access_count ASC)
8. Save updated state_file
9. Send Telegram digest

DIGEST FORMAT:
🧠 Memory Decay Report — [DATE]

{count} files untouched for 30+ days:
1. [filename] — {days} days stale | {access_count} accesses | {word_count} words
   Suggestion: [ARCHIVE/KEEP/MERGE]

{count} low-value files (short + rarely accessed):
1. [filename] — {word_count} words, accessed {access_count}x
   Suggestion: MERGE into a related file or DELETE

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/.openclaw/memory/ directory if it does not exist
Initialize ~/.openclaw/memory/memory_decay_state.json with {"files": {}, "archive_candidates": []}
Create script: ~/scripts/memory_decay.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/memory_decay.sh
Add to crontab: "0 9 * * 1" to run every Monday morning
Test: run manually and review the decay report
Verify Telegram notifications work
prompts.example_output:

   Suggestion: ARCHIVE — project is inactive
2. memory/2025-12-ideas.md — 38 days stale | 1 access | 80 words
   Suggestion: MERGE — small file, can combine with memory/2026-01-ideas.md
3. memory/2026-01-old-process.md — 32 days stale | 5 accesses | 340 words
   Suggestion: KEEP — referenced often, update before archiving

1 low-value file:

prompts.title: Duplicate Detective
prompts.description: Scans your memory files for near-duplicate entries and proposes merges — keeps your knowledge base clean and avoids fragmented thinking.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that scans your memory files for near-duplicate entries and proposes merges.

CONFIGURATION:
- memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/
- similarity_threshold: 0.75 (75% similarity = candidate)
- output_file: ~/logs/duplicates_report.txt

PROCESS:
1. List all *.md files in ~/.openclaw/memory/ recursively
2. For each pair of files (or sections within files):
   a. Read file content
   b. Normalize: lowercase, remove punctuation, collapse whitespace
   c. Calculate simple similarity: Jaccard index of word sets
   d. Also check for: same header/title, same date prefix, shared key phrases
3. Flag any pair with similarity > 0.75 as a DUPLICATE_CANDIDATE
4. For each duplicate candidate:
   - Identify the PRIMARY (older/more complete) vs DUPLICATE (newer/shorter)
   - Extract: which paragraphs differ, which are identical
   - Propose a MERGE recommendation
5. Write report to output_file
6. Send Telegram summary if duplicates found

REPORT FORMAT:
🔍 Duplicate Detective Report — [DATE]

Found {count} duplicate pairs:

[{count}]. [file-A.md] vs [file-B.md]
    Similarity: {XX%} | Created: {date} vs {date}
    Overlap: {description of shared content}
    Recommendation: MERGE into [PRIMARY], delete [DUPLICATE]
    Merge plan:
      - Keep: [paragraph from A]
      - Add from B: [paragraph from B not in A]
      - Delete: [duplicate paragraphs]

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Verify memory directory and list current files
Create script: ~/scripts/duplicate_detective.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/duplicate_detective.sh
Add to crontab: "0 10 * * 1" to run every Monday at 10 AM
Test: run manually and review proposed merges
For each merge: manually review before deleting duplicates
prompts.example_output:
🔍 Duplicate Detective Report — Apr 11 2026

Found 2 duplicate pairs:

[1]. memory/2026-03-tasks.md vs memory/TASKS.md
    Similarity: 82% | Created: Mar 15 vs Mar 20
    Overlap: Both contain the same project milestones section
    Recommendation: MERGE into memory/2026-03-tasks.md (older, more complete), archive TASKS.md
    Merge plan:
      - Keep: Full project milestones from 2026-03-tasks.md
      - Add from TASKS.md: [Daily standup notes section not in primary]
      - Delete: Duplicate project overview paragraphs

prompts.title: Memory Usage Leaderboard
prompts.description: Ranks which memory files get accessed the most, so you know what is actually useful versus sitting there untouched — guides what to promote to long-term memory.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that tracks how often each memory file is accessed and ranks them by usefulness — so you know what to promote to long-term memory and what to archive.

CONFIGURATION:
- memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/
- access_log: ~/.openclaw/memory/access_log.jsonl
- leaderboard_file: ~/.openclaw/memory/usage_leaderboard.json
- lookback_days: 30

ACCESS LOG FORMAT (one JSON object per line):
{"timestamp": "ISO8601", "file": "memory/path/file.md", "action": "read", "bytes_read": 1024}

PROCESS:
1. Ensure access_log.jsonl exists in ~/.openclaw/memory/
2. Filter access_log to last {lookback_days} days
3. Group by file: for each unique file, count total accesses and sum bytes_read
4. Calculate:
   - access_count: number of times opened
   - total_bytes: total bytes read
   - last_accessed: most recent access timestamp
   - access_rate: accesses per week
5. Rank files by access_count DESC
6. Identify:
   - HOT: top 5 by access count
   - COLD: bottom 5 by access count (excluding files created in last 7 days)
   - GROWING: files with access_count increasing week-over-week
   - DECLINING: files with access_count decreasing week-over-week
7. Save leaderboard to leaderboard_file
8. Send Telegram leaderboard digest

DIGEST FORMAT:
🏆 Memory Leaderboard — Last 30 Days

📈 HOTTEST (Most Accessed):
  1. memory/todos.md — {count} accesses | avg {X}KB/read
  2. memory/project-a.md — {count} accesses | avg {X}KB/read

❄️ COLDEST (Least Accessed):
  1. memory/old-notes.md — {count} accesses — consider archiving

📊 TRENDS:
  🚀 Growing: memory/quarterly-planning.md (+40% vs last period)
  📉 Declining: memory/legacy-arch.md (-60% vs last period)

💡 RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Promote HOT files to long-term memory
- Archive or delete COLD files not referenced in 30+ days

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Ensure access_log.jsonl exists: touch ~/.openclaw/memory/access_log.jsonl
Create script: ~/scripts/memory_leaderboard.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/memory_leaderboard.sh
Add to crontab: "0 8 * * *" to run every morning at 8 AM
Instrument your memory reads to log to access_log.jsonl (wrap fs reads with logging)
Test: run manually and review the leaderboard

prompts.title: Cron Watchdog
prompts.description: Checks system cron logs every 15 minutes, alerts immediately if a scheduled job missed or errored out — keeps your automation reliable.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that checks system cron logs every 15 minutes and alerts immediately if a scheduled job missed or errored out.

CONFIGURATION:
- log_file: ~/logs/cron_watchdog.log
- alert_threshold: 1 (alert on first error)
- known_jobs: list of expected cron job identifiers

PROCESS:
1. Run: grep -i "error\|failed\|missed\|no crontab" /var/log/syslog 2>/dev/null OR grep -r "ERROR\|FAILED" ~/logs/*.log 2>/dev/null
2. Also check: crontab -l to list active jobs, compare against known_jobs
3. For each error found:
   a. Extract: timestamp, job name, error message
   b. Classify severity: WARNING (non-critical) vs ERROR (job failed) vs MISSED (job did not run)
4. Log all findings to ~/logs/cron_watchdog.log with timestamp
5. If any ERROR or MISSED found:
   a. Send Telegram alert immediately
   b. Include: job name, error message, timestamp, suggested fix

ALERT FORMAT:
🐕 Cron Watchdog Alert — [TIMESTAMP]

🚨 Job Failed: [job_name]
Command: [cron command]
Error: [error message]
Last run: [timestamp]

💡 Fix: [suggested action]

✅ OK jobs: [list of jobs that ran successfully]

TOOLS: exec, cron, filesystem, message
setup_steps: Create ~/logs/ directory if not exists
Verify cron is running: ps aux | grep cron
Create script: ~/scripts/cron_watchdog.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/cron_watchdog.sh
Add to crontab: "*/15 * * * *" ~/scripts/cron_watchdog.sh
Test: intentionally break a cron job, verify alert fires within 15 minutes

prompts.title: SSL Expiry Tracker
prompts.description: Monitors SSL certificate expiration dates across all your domains, pings you 30 days, 7 days, and 1 day before expiry — no more surprise certificate outages.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that monitors SSL certificate expiration dates across all your domains and alerts you 30 days, 7 days, and 1 day before expiry.

CONFIGURATION:
Create ~/configs/ssl_domains.json:
{
  "domains": [
    {"name": "decryptica.com", "port": 443},
    {"name": "api.decryptica.com", "port": 443}
  ],
  "alert_days": [30, 7, 1],
  "state_file": "~/.openclaw/ssl_state.json"
}

PROCESS:
1. Load domains from config
2. For each domain:
   a. Run: openssl s_client -connect DOMAIN:PORT -servername DOMAIN 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates 2>/dev/null
   b. Extract: notBefore (issue date), notAfter (expiry date)
   c. Calculate: days_until_expiry = (notAfter - now) / 86400
3. Load state_file
4. For each domain:
   - If days_until_expiry in [30, 7, 1]: and not yet alerted at this threshold → trigger alert
   - Update state_file with latest check timestamp and expiry dates
5. If days_until_expiry <= 0: CRITICAL — certificate has expired
6. Send appropriate Telegram alert

ALERT FORMAT (30/7 day warning):
🔒 SSL Expiry Warning — [DOMAIN]

⚠️ Certificate for [DOMAIN] expires in [X] days
Expires: [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC]
Issued: [YYYY-MM-DD]

💡 Action: Renew certificate before [date]

ALERT FORMAT (1 day / CRITICAL):
🔒🔒 CRITICAL: SSL Certificate EXPIRED — [DOMAIN]

Certificate expired [X] days ago!
Renew immediately to restore HTTPS.

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/ssl_domains.json with your domains and ports
Test manually: openssl s_client -connect yourdomain.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
Create script: ~/scripts/ssl_expiry.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/ssl_expiry.sh
Add to crontab: "0 9 * * *" to check every morning at 9 AM
Verify Telegram bot is configured

    "p95_latency_1h": 480,
    "alert_sent_today": false
  }
}

PROCESS:
1. Load endpoints from config and history from history_file
2. For each endpoint:
   a. Run: curl -o /dev/null -s -w "latency_ms:%{time_total} http_code:%{http_code}" [URL]
   b. Record: timestamp, latency_ms, http_status
   c. Append to checks[] array in history (keep last 1000 entries)
3. Calculate:
   - p50, p95, p99 latency from last 1 hour
   - Error rate: % of non-200 responses in last hour
   - Trend: p95_latency_1h vs p95_latency_previous_hour
4. Check thresholds:
   - If any latency > threshold_ms AND alert_sent_today == false → ALERT
   - If error rate > 5% → ALERT
   - If p95 degraded > 50% vs previous hour → DEGRADATION ALERT
5. Send Telegram alert
6. Update state and reset alert_sent_today at midnight

ALERT FORMAT:
📉 API Latency Alert — [ENDPOINT_NAME]

🔴 Current latency: [X]ms (threshold: [Y]ms)
📊 p95 (1h): [X]ms | p99 (1h): [X]ms
⚠️ Error rate: [X]% (5xx count: [N])
📉 Trend: p95 +[X]% vs last hour

💡 Check: [endpoint] may be overloaded or experiencing issues.

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/api_endpoints.json with your endpoints, names, and timeout thresholds
Test manually: curl each endpoint and verify you get latency and status codes
Create script: ~/scripts/api_latency.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/api_latency.sh
Add to crontab: "*/5 * * * *" ~/scripts/api_latency.sh
Test degradation alert: artificially slow an endpoint and verify alert fires within 10 minutes
prompts.example_output:
📉 API Latency Alert — Helius RPC

🔴 Current latency: 4890ms (threshold: 3000ms)
📊 p95 (1h): 3200ms | p99 (1h): 5100ms
⚠️ Error rate: 8.3% (5xx count: 14)
📉 Trend: p95 +67% vs last hour

💡 Check: Helius RPC may be overloaded or experiencing upstream issues.

prompts.title: Bot Wallet Health Check
prompts.description: Monitors your sniper/scaler bot wallets for balance changes, large transactions, failed swaps, and suspicious activity — alerts same-channel when something looks off.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that monitors your sniper and scaler bot wallets for balance changes, large transactions, failed swaps, and suspicious activity — alerts on the same channel when something looks wrong.

CONFIGURATION:
Create ~/configs/wallet_config.json:
{
  "wallets": [
    {"name": "Sniper Bot", "address": "5NPSXWar19xrmHTsELnRjnbH13T9NVGMPiQ4nUe621vu", "chain": "solana"},
    {"name": "Polygon Wallet", "address": "0x28aD2F2B72eC57c05Bd03a33080390235D57C92c", "chain": "polygon"}
  ],
  "alert_threshold_usd": 100,
  "state_file": "~/.openclaw/wallet_state.json"
}

WALLET STATE:
{
  "5NPSXWar19xrmHTsELnRjnbH13T9NVGMPiQ4nUe621vu": {
    "last_balance": 0.8,
    "last_check": 1744156800,
    "last_txs": ["tx1hash", "tx2hash"],
    "alert_sent": false
  }
}

PROCESS:
1. For each wallet:
   a. Fetch current balance using appropriate RPC/API:
      - Solana: curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"getBalance","params":["ADDRESS"]}' https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com
      - Polygon: curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_getBalance","params":["ADDRESS","latest"]}' https://polygon-rpc.com
   b. Fetch recent transactions (last 10): use blockchain explorer API or RPC
   c. Compare to last_check state:
      - Balance change > alert_threshold_usd in value → LARGE CHANGE ALERT
      - New tx not in last_txs → NEW TRANSACTION (check if expected)
      - Failed tx (status: failed) → FAILED TX ALERT
      - Balance decreased significantly with no tx → SUSPICIOUS
2. Update state_file with new balances and tx hashes
3. Send Telegram alert for any anomalies

ALERT FORMAT:
💰 Bot Wallet Alert — [WALLET_NAME]

🔴 LARGE BALANCE CHANGE
Before: [X] SOL | After: [Y] SOL
Change: [+-Z] SOL ([$VALUE])

⚠️ NEW TRANSACTION:
Type: [swap/send/receive] | Amount: [X]
TX: [explorer link]

🔴 FAILED TX:
TX: [hash]
Error: [reason if available]

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/wallet_config.json with your wallet addresses and chains
Get RPC endpoints for each chain (public or private RPCs)
Create script: ~/scripts/wallet_health.sh
Make executable: chmod +x ~/scripts/wallet_health.sh
Add to crontab: "*/15 * * * *" ~/scripts/wallet_health.sh
Test manually: run script and verify it reads correct balances
Verify Telegram bot sends to the correct channel
prompts.example_output:
💰 Bot Wallet Alert — Sniper Bot

🔴 LARGE BALANCE CHANGE
Before: 0.8 SOL | After: 0.32 SOL
Change: -0.48 SOL ($87.20)

⚠️ NEW TRANSACTION:
Type: JUP swap | Amount: 0.48 SOL → [token]
TX: https://solscan.io/tx/xxxxx

⚠️ NOTE: This matches a recent sniper transaction. OK if expected.

prompts.title: Memory Decay Detector
prompts.description: Flags memory files untouched for 30+ days, suggests what is stale and ready to archive.
prompts.prompt_text:
Set up an always-on automation that scans your memory files, flags anything untouched for 30+ days, and suggests what is stale and ready to archive.

CONFIG: memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/, threshold_days: 30, state_file: ~/.openclaw/memory/memory_decay_state.json

PROCESS: 1. List all .md files in memory dir 2. For each file get last modified timestamp 3. Flag files where days_since_access > 30 as STALE 4. Also flag files with word_count < 50 and access_count == 1 as LOW_VALUE 5. Build archive_candidates list sorted by staleness 6. Save to state_file 7. Send Telegram digest

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create memory directories and state file
Create ~/scripts/memory_decay.sh
chmod +x and add to crontab weekly
Test manually and verify Telegram
prompts.example_output: Memory Decay Report: 3 files untouched for 30+ days. Archive candidates: memory/old-notes.md (47 days stale), memory/deprecated-process.md (32 days stale). Low-value: memory/2025-10-note.md (22 words, 1 access).

prompts.title: Duplicate Detective
prompts.description: Scans memory files for near-duplicate entries and proposes merges — keeps knowledge base clean.
prompts.prompt_text:
Scan your memory files for near-duplicate entries and propose merges.

CONFIG: memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/, similarity_threshold: 0.75

PROCESS: 1. List all .md files 2. For each pair: normalize text, calculate Jaccard similarity 3. Flag pairs with similarity > 0.75 4. Identify primary (older/more complete) vs duplicate 5. Propose merge plan 6. Write report and send Telegram if duplicates found

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Create ~/scripts/duplicate_detective.sh
chmod +x and add to crontab weekly
Test manually, review proposed merges before deleting
prompts.example_output: Duplicate Detective Report: Found 2 duplicate pairs. [1] memory/2026-03-tasks.md vs memory/TASKS.md (82% similar). Recommendation: MERGE into memory/2026-03-tasks.md, archive TASKS.md.

prompts.title: Memory Usage Leaderboard
prompts.description: Ranks which memory files get accessed the most — guides what to promote to long-term memory.
prompts.prompt_text:
Track how often each memory file is accessed and rank them by usefulness.

CONFIG: memory_dir: ~/.openclaw/memory/, access_log: ~/.openclaw/memory/access_log.jsonl, lookback_days: 30

PROCESS: 1. Filter access_log to last 30 days 2. Group by file, count accesses and bytes_read 3. Rank by access_count DESC 4. Identify HOT (top 5) and COLD (bottom 5) 5. Identify GROWING vs DECLINING trends 6. Save leaderboard and send Telegram digest

TOOLS: exec, filesystem, cron, message
setup_steps: Ensure access_log.jsonl exists
Create ~/scripts/memory_leaderboard.sh
Add to crontab daily
Instrument memory reads to log to access_log
prompts.example_output: Memory Leaderboard (30 days): HOTTEST: memory/todos.md (47 accesses), memory/project-a.md (31 accesses). COLDEST: memory/old-2024-notes.md (0 accesses). TRENDS: memory/quarterly-planning.md +40% vs last period.

prompts.title: Onboarding Memory Seeder
prompts.description: Creates a fresh memory scaffold for a new team member from your existing knowledge base.
prompts.prompt_text:
Take an existing knowledge base and automatically generate a complete onboarding memory scaffold for a new team member.

Generate ONBOARDING_name.md: welcome, key projects, immediate priorities, key contacts 3. Generate CONTEXT_name.md: company background, relevant history, active decisions 4. Generate QUICKSTART_name.md: top 5 things to do first week, key files to read, whom to talk to first, gotchas 5. Save to ~/.openclaw/memory/onboarding/

prompts.title: Cron Watchdog
prompts.description: Checks system cron logs every 15 minutes, alerts immediately if a scheduled job missed or errored out.
prompts.prompt_text:
Check system cron logs every 15 minutes and alert immediately if a scheduled job missed or errored out.

CONFIG: log_file: ~/logs/cron_watchdog.log

PROCESS: 1. Run: grep -i "error|failed|missed" on system logs or ~/logs/*.log 2. Compare active crontab against known jobs 3. For each error: extract timestamp, job name, error message 4. Classify severity: WARNING vs ERROR vs MISSED 5. Log to ~/logs/cron_watchdog.log 6. If ERROR or MISSED: send Telegram alert immediately

ALERT FORMAT:
Cron Watchdog Alert: Job Failed: [job_name]. Command: [cron command]. Error: [message]. Last run: [timestamp]. Fix: [suggested action]
setup_steps: Create ~/logs/ directory
Create ~/scripts/cron_watchdog.sh
chmod +x
Add to crontab: */15 * * * *
Test by intentionally breaking a cron job
prompts.example_output: Cron Watchdog Alert: Job Failed: nightly-backup. Exit code: 1. Error: disk quota exceeded. Fix: Delete backups older than 30 days. OK jobs: heartbeat-monitor, memory-consolidation.

prompts.title: SSL Expiry Tracker
prompts.description: Monitors SSL certificate expiration dates across domains, pings you 30 days, 7 days, and 1 day before expiry.
prompts.prompt_text:
Monitor SSL certificate expiration dates across all your domains and alert at 30 days, 7 days, and 1 day before expiry.

CONFIG: ~/configs/ssl_domains.json with domains array and alert_days: [30, 7, 1]

PROCESS: 1. For each domain: run openssl s_client to get certificate dates 2. Calculate days_until_expiry 3. If days in alert_days threshold and not yet alerted: trigger alert 4. If days <= 0: CRITICAL — certificate expired 5. Send Telegram alert

ALERT FORMAT:
SSL Expiry Warning: [DOMAIN] expires in [X] days. Expires: [YYYY-MM-DD]. Action: Renew certificate. (1 day alert: CRITICAL: EXPIRED — renew immediately)
setup_steps: Create ~/configs/ssl_domains.json with your domains
Test: openssl s_client -connect yourdomain.com:443
Create ~/scripts/ssl_expiry.sh
Add to crontab daily
prompts.example_output: SSL Expiry Warning: decryptica.com expires in 7 days (2026-04-18). Action: Renew certificate before Apr 18 to avoid outage.

prompts.title: Scheduled Script Runner

Run any custom script on a cron schedule, log all output, alert via Telegram if the script fails.

CONFIG: ~/configs/script_runner.json with jobs array (name, script, schedule, timeout_minutes)

PROCESS: 1. Read jobs from config 2. For each job: create log file, run with timeout, capture exit code 3. If exit != 0 or timeout: send Telegram alert with error summary 4. Alert if job missed (not run for 2x schedule interval)

Script Failed: [JOB_NAME]. Exit code: [X] (timeout: YES/NO). Last 20 lines of log: [excerpt]. Fix: [suggested action].

prompts.title: Codebase Q&A Bot
prompts.description: Ask anything about a codebase, it reads the files and answers — grounded in actual code, no hallucinating.
prompts.prompt_text:
Answer questions about a codebase by reading the actual files.

INPUT: project_path, question

PROCESS: 1. Use find and grep to locate relevant files 2. Read files with read tool 3. Answer with: file paths, line numbers, direct quotes from code 4. If not found in code: say explicitly "I could not find this in the codebase" 5. Include relevant code snippets (max 20 lines)

RULES: Never make up file paths, function names, or line numbers. If unsure, say so.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Answer
[Direct answer]
## Evidence
File: [path]:[line]
[code snippet]
## Confidence
[High/Medium/Low]
setup_steps: Provide project path and question
Model searches actual codebase files
Answers are always grounded in real code

Email Digest (N unread): Sender: Subject. Preview: snippet. Time: relative. [Repeat]
